title: No-Churn Vanilla Ice Cream
subtitle: Double cream and condensed milk frozen without an ice cream maker
excerpt: Sweetened condensed milk folded through whipped double cream with vanilla and frozen in a container. No machine, no custard, no churning. The condensed milk prevents ice crystals forming and keeps the texture smooth. Three ingredients and twenty minutes of work.
sourceNotes: The no-churn ice cream method using condensed milk and whipped cream became widely known in British home cooking in the latter twentieth century. The condensed milk acts as both sweetener and cryoprotectant, preventing the large ice crystal formation that makes hand-frozen ice cream icy and granular.

recipe.freezeNotes: Store in the freezer for up to one month. Transfer to the fridge for 15 minutes before scooping.
recipe.batchNotes: Make multiple batches at the same time, they require no special equipment and freeze independently.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Must be made at least 6 hours ahead to freeze solid.

content.text: No-churn ice cream works because condensed milk has a very high sugar content, which lowers the freezing point of the mixture and prevents the large ice crystals that would make a hand-frozen ice cream icy and gritty. The whipped cream introduces air, giving the frozen result a lighter texture. The method is simpler than it sounds: whip, fold, freeze.

The cream must be whipped to firm peaks, not soft. Soft-peak cream folded with condensed milk will give a dense, heavy ice cream rather than a light one. Fold gently so as not to deflate the air you have just beaten in. The mixture goes into the freezer immediately, don't let it sit at room temperature or it will start to melt together and lose structure.
A freezer-proof container with a lid, about 1.5 litres capacity. Metal containers freeze faster and give a better texture; plastic is fine.
Whip {{double-cream}} of double cream with a hand mixer until it holds firm peaks. The cream should stand up in stiff mounds when the beaters are lifted.

Pour {{condensed-milk}} of condensed milk and {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract over the whipped cream. Fold gently with a large spatula or metal spoon in a figure-of-eight motion until just combined. The mixture should be thick and uniform without any visible streaks of condensed milk.

Pour immediately into a lidded freezer-proof container. Smooth the surface, cover, and freeze for at least 6 hours until fully set. The ice cream is ready when a metal spoon pressed into the surface meets resistance.

items.symptom: Ice cream is icy and crystalline
items.cause: Cream was not whipped to firm enough peaks, or the condensed milk was not a full tin
items.fix: Whip to firm peaks, the cream should hold its shape firmly. Use a full 397 g tin of condensed milk. A smaller amount means less cryoprotection.

items.symptom: Ice cream is very dense and heavy
items.cause: Cream was over-whipped and the air collapsed, or folded too vigorously
items.fix: Fold gently with as few strokes as possible. The cream should still look airy after the condensed milk is incorporated.

items.cause: Condensed milk is inherently very sweet
items.fix: Add a squeeze of lemon juice to the mixture before freezing to balance the sweetness. A pinch of sea salt also helps.

items.symptom: Ice cream didn't set firm after 6 hours
items.cause: Freezer is not cold enough, or the container was too full and didn't freeze evenly
items.fix: Make sure the freezer is at -18°C or below. A very large or very full container may need 8+ hours. Transfer to a smaller container if needed.

content.text: No-churn ice cream using condensed milk became widely known in British home cooking during the latter decades of the twentieth century, when ice cream makers were not standard kitchen equipment. The condensed milk method solves the structural problem of hand-frozen ice cream, the large ice crystals that form without churning, by using concentrated sugar to lower the freezing point. Versions of the recipe appear regularly in British food magazines from the 1980s onward and have remained popular as an accessible alternative to the custard-based method.
